items.description: Public type `Greeter` with `greet(name: String) -> String` returning "Hello, <name>!" and `greet() -> String` returning "Hello, world!".

rules_checked.reasoning: The item declares kind 'type' and describes a concrete public struct/class Greeter with two methods (greet(name:) and greet()). This is exactly what a 'type' declaration is — a concrete data/behavior unit, not a protocol, extension, or free function. Kind matches the actual thing being built.

Target Sources/Greeter/Greeter.swift lives in the Greeter module, which Package.swift defines as the library target (name: "Greeter", target(name: "Greeter")). The existing Sources/Greeter/GreeterModule.swift scaffold confirms this is the correct home for the module's core public type.

There is no view/view-model/service layering in this scaffold-stage package; Greeter is a plain, self-contained type performing simple string formatting with no data access or UI logic, so no altitude violation applies.

A single new type is warranted: there is no existing type in the module to extend (only a namespace marker enum GreeterModule exists), and the two required behaviors (greet with/without name) are cohesive single-responsibility operations of one Greeter type — not split into unnecessary extra types nor crammed into an unrelated one.

No protocol is introduced by this item — only a concrete type with two methods. No speculative abstraction is present, and none is needed since there's no stated substitution or test-seam requirement.

`Greeter` is a noun naming a concrete role-performing type; `greet(name:)` and `greet()` are verb-named methods with a clear labeled argument, following Swift API Design Guidelines (methods that perform actions are verbs, argument labels read naturally at call site).

The item introduces a single new file within the Greeter module with no imports or dependencies described; nothing crosses module boundaries or smuggles a forbidden import, consistent with the declared_scope which only touches Sources/Greeter, Tests/GreeterTests, and docs/features/F-M1.
